Why large clouds failed (helix)

A regime-spanning crash in the energy phase for massive clouds — the mechanism, why only the massive ones, and the fix.

⚠️ This document may be out of date — verify before trusting it. It is a point-in-time analysis/audit, not a maintained spec; the code moves faster than these notes (paths, line numbers, and “what shipped” status drift). Any agent or person reading this: treat it as unverified — re-check each claim, snippet, and line reference against the current source before relying on it.

🔄 Living book — recheck and refine on every visit. This is an evolving narrative, not a frozen record. Whoever opens it should re-verify the claims and line references against current source, update anything that has drifted, and rethink the arc itself if a better ordering or framing exists — leave it better than you found it.

💾 Persist diagnostics — commit, don't re-run. The container is ephemeral and the full/hybr runs cost hours; every figure and number here traces to a committed harness/CSV in the relevant docs/dev/<workstream>/ folder, so a future visit reproduces or compares without re-running.

Chapter 1Failed large clouds — diagnosis & fix

TRINITY feedback-bubble code · energy-driven phase · diagnosis & minimal robustness fix

docs/dev/failed-large-clouds/ · point-in-time analysis (2026-06-19)

Massive giant molecular clouds crashed TRINITY's energy phase with Eb=nan / R1 root finding failed. This report walks the full arc: the symptom, the numerical mechanism, the physical reason it only hits massive clouds, the ideas we tested, the one we shipped across every affected config, and the honest list of claims we had to revise along the way.

RESOLVED All three failing configs now terminate cleanly (ENERGY_COLLAPSED, code 51); healthy configs are a byte-identical no-op; pytest 555 passed. Fix = G (geometry guard) + F (graceful collapse termination), ~93 lines across 4 files + a 6-case test.

1 · The problem

For a giant-molecular-cloud mass mCloud, star-formation efficiency sfe, and ambient density nCore, TRINITY integrates an expanding feedback bubble through its phases. A band of massive, dense clouds — e.g. mCloud=5e9, sfe=0.1, nCore=1e2, and the real Helix point (sfe=0.05, PISM=0) — did not just give a wrong answer. They crashed: a ValueError traceback out of the bubble-pressure solve, or silent nan propagating into the saved outputs.

A crash on a physically-plausible input is a correctness bug: a sweep over cloud masses hits these cells and dies, or worse, records nan and continues.

2 · The mechanism — how a crash falls out of the math

The interior bubble pressure is set by the thermal energy $E_b$ divided by the hot-gas (shell) volume between the wind shock $R_1$ and the outer shell $R_2$:

$$P_b = (\gamma-1)\,\frac{E_b}{V},\qquad V=\frac{4\pi}{3}\left(R_2^{3}-R_1^{3}\right).$$

When a run drives $E_b \to 0$, the inner-shock solver (solve_R1) pushes $R_1 \to R_2$, so the shell volume collapses, $V \to 0$, and

$$P_b=(\gamma-1)\frac{E_b}{V}\;\longrightarrow\;\frac{0}{0}\;\longrightarrow\;\text{NaN / }\tfrac{1}{0},$$

which then poisons the next solve (a $\sqrt{R_2}$ with $R_2<0$ in get_r1, or a cooling-table out-of-bounds at $E_b\approx 0$) and raises. Figure 3 shows the geometry of the collapse and where the old code died versus where the fix now stops.

3 · The real question — why does $E_b\to0$ here?

Stopping the divide-by-zero treats the symptom. The diagnosis needed the cause: what term in the energy budget drains $E_b$? The energy-phase ODE (energy_phase_ODEs.py) is

$$\frac{dE_b}{dt}=\underbrace{L_{\rm mech}}_{\text{wind+SN in}} -\underbrace{L_{\rm cool}}_{\text{radiative}} -\underbrace{4\pi R_2^{2}\,P_b\,v_2}_{\text{PdV work on the shell}} -\underbrace{L_{\rm leak}}_{\text{cover-fraction leak (}C_f<1\text{)}}.$$

The first hypothesis was catastrophic cooling. Decomposing the budget from the run snapshots falsified it: cooling is ~1% of the mechanical input ($L_{\rm cool}/L_{\rm mech}\approx0.01$), while the PdV expansion work climbs from 0.52 to 1.56 of $L_{\rm mech}$ and crosses unity exactly at the $E_b$ peak. The bubble is doing more expansion work than feedback puts in — so its energy peaks and collapses.

4 · Why only massive clouds — healthy vs failing

The discriminator is the shell velocity, since $\text{PdV}\propto v_2$. A massive, concentrated cluster launches the shell at ~2000–3700 km/s — near free-expansion ($R\approx v t$), out of the self-similar Weaver equilibrium where PdV is a fixed sub-unity fraction of $L_{\rm mech}$. A healthy cloud stays Weaver-like: $\text{PdV}/L_{\rm mech}<1$, the shell decelerates, and $E_b$ grows.

A verified side-result: the massive cloud's energy phase starts ~70× later

With $t_{\rm SF}=0$ (logged), the energy phase begins at $t_0=\Delta t_{\rm fs}$, the free-streaming duration:

$$t_0=\Delta t_{\rm fs}=\sqrt{\frac{3\dot M}{4\pi\,\rho_a\,v_0^{3}}}\;\propto\;\sqrt{M_{\rm cluster}}$$

because $\rho_a$ (same nCore) and the wind terminal velocity $v_0=2L_w/\dot p_w=3739\,$pc/Myr (mass-independent) are shared, while $\dot M\propto M_{\rm cluster}$ (logged $\dot M_{\rm wind}$: 1.451 vs $2.901\times10^{-4}\,M_\odot$/yr = ratio 5000). So the $t_0$ ratio is $\sqrt{5000}=70.71$, matching the logged $1.383\times10^{-3}/1.956\times10^{-5}=70.71$ exactly. The same scaling explains the IC pressure: $P_{b,0}\propto E_0/r_0^3\propto L_w^2\rho_a/\dot p_w^2\propto\rho_a=n_{\rm core}\mu$ — set by ambient density and the wind, not by cluster mass.

Were they ever genuinely energy-driven? (measured, 5 configs)

This is the question that decides whether including PdV in a transition trigger is physically natural or a band-aid. The cleanest signal is the reservoir growth $E_b^{\rm peak}/E_b^{\rm init}$ — a pure state variable, fully reliable. Healthy controls build the hot-bubble thermal reservoir by $\ge\times$39,300–94,900 (lower bounds — still energy-driven at the $t\le1$ Myr window cap); all three failing configs build it by $\times$1.014 (~1%). The energy-driven reservoir essentially never forms. The nuance: it is not "PdV>1 from birth" — every config starts at the same self-similar handoff $\text{PdV}/L_{\rm mech}\approx0.5$. The fork is the direction: healthy clouds decelerate, so PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ falls and the reservoir builds; failing clouds never decelerate, so PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ rises through 1 within ~7–10% of the phase and $E_b$ collapses.

Interpretation. The failing clouds are "stillborn" energy-driven bubbles: they inherit the energy-driven initial condition but never establish the self-similar deceleration that defines the phase. So a PdV-inclusive transition trigger would be detecting "this bubble failed to become energy-driven" — a regime mismatch, not a healthy Weaver→momentum transition. That argues the deeper-correct fix is to recognise these as free-expansion/momentum-dominated earlier, not to bolt a PdV term onto the cooling-based transition test.

5 · Ideas tested

Two layers were kept distinct: cheap numeric guards (does just stopping the divide-by-zero suffice?) and the fix families a production change would choose from.

Numeric-guard variants (monkeypatched in the harness)

idpatchhypothesis
V0baseline (no patch)crashes on the band (reference)
V1clamp $R_1\le R_2(1-\varepsilon)$ in solve_R1kills the inf
V2floor shell volume $R_2^3-R_1^3\ge\varepsilon R_2^3$ in bubble_E2Psame, via the divide site
V3V1 + V2combined guard

Fix families

idfamilywhat it changesrole
Ggeometry guard$R_1<R_2$ / volume floor so the divide can't blow upnecessary safety net — not sufficient alone
Ccancellation-free $P_b$the get_r1 identity removes the catastrophic cancellation at its sourceoptional conditioning
Floud / graceful failisfinite gate ⇒ clean termination with a reasonbelt-and-suspenders for any NaN source
Ttransition (deferred)detect the PdV-inclusive net-energy zero-crossing ⇒ hand off to the momentum phasethe physically-complete end-state
Key empirical result — geometry guard alone is NOT enough. Smoke-testing V3 on fail_repro: with the geometry clamped, the ODE keeps integrating and $E_b$ crosses through zero into negative ($+7.4\times10^{8}\to-9.1\times10^{8}\to-1.0\times10^{12}$), giving negative $P_b$; the bubble solve then has no physical root, fsolve thrashes, and the run never terminates (SIGTERM'd at 320 s). So a guard must be paired with a graceful stop (F).

6 · One idea across configs

The shipped G+F fix was exercised across the whole affected band and the healthy controls. The Helix point collapses inside phase 1a (where per-segment solves raise before the end-of-loop check), which forced a second round of coverage: solve_R1 returns 0.0 for non-physical $R_2\le0$, and the phase-1a bubble solve is wrapped so any degenerate-collapse exception becomes a clean ENERGY_COLLAPSED.

configcollapse phasecrashedend statefinal $R_2$ [pc]
fail_repro (sfe0.1 / PISM1e4)1bFalseENERGY_COLLAPSED (51)9.73
fail_pism6 (sfe0.1 / PISM1e6)1bFalseENERGY_COLLAPSED (51)9.73
fail_helix (real Helix: sfe0.05 / PISM0)1aFalseENERGY_COLLAPSED (51)7.03
small_1e5 / small_1e6 (healthy)—Falseno-op (byte-identical to pre-fix)—

7 · Performance & gates

gatebarresult
Robustnesscrashed=False on the whole failing band✅ all stop with code 51
No-ophealthy outputs identical to pre-fix✅ byte-identical dictionary.jsonl
Unitguard fires only when degenerate; pinned✅ 6/6, incl. a bit-identity pin
Regressionfull suite green✅ pytest 555 passed

The volume floor is written so its branch is dead while the volume is positive — i.e. provably bit-identical for every physical bubble — and that invariant is pinned by test_bubble_E2P_bit_identical_when_volume_positive. There is no runtime cost on healthy runs.

8 · The solution

Shipped: G + F. Family T (momentum handoff) is deferred to docs/dev/transition/ per maintainer decision; until then collapsed points are cleanly flagged for sweeps to filter.

  1. G — bubble_E2P volume floor (get_bubbleParams.py:226-235): if (r2³−r1³) <= 0: shell_volume = 1e-13·r2³. Stops the divide-by-zero at its source.
  2. F — collapse ⇒ clean stop in both energy phases (run_energy_phase.py, run_energy_implicit_phase.py): when not isfinite(Eb) or Eb <= 0, raise EndSimulationDirectly with the new SimulationEndCode.ENERGY_COLLAPSED ((51,"energy_collapsed"), the 50–59 "inspection-required" band). main.py then skips the later phases via the existing gate.
  3. Phase-1a coverage: solve_R1 returns 0.0 for non-physical $R_2\le0$, and the phase-1a bubble solve is wrapped so a degenerate-collapse exception becomes ENERGY_COLLAPSED rather than a traceback.

Total: ~93 lines across 4 files + test/test_energy_collapse_guard.py (6 cases). The cooling_balance transition is untouched.

9 · Corrections log

Four assertions were made and then revised during the analysis — each settled by a specific source or measurement, recorded here so the originals are not re-trusted.

  1. "The failure is catastrophic cooling." → PdV expansion work, not cooling. Settled by the $dE_b/dt$ budget (Fig 1): $L_{\rm cool}/L_{\rm mech}\approx0.01$; $\text{PdV}/L_{\rm mech}$ runs 0.52→1.56, crossing 1 at the $E_b$ peak.
  2. "Snapshot 0's $P_b$ is a fixed seed/placeholder." → The genuine Weaver initial condition, computed $P_b=\texttt{bubble\_E2P}(E_0,r_0,R_1)$ (run_energy_phase.py:97-100).
  3. "$P_{b,0}$ is bit-identical across the two clouds." → ≈equal to ~6 sig figs ($2.135768\times10^7$ vs $2.135766\times10^7$): $P_{b,0}\propto n_{\rm core}$ and both share nCore=1e2, so it is near-equal, not bit-identical.
  4. "The healthy run starts later (a delay)." → A plotting artifact. reliable_mask had trimmed the $v_2$/$E_b$ state too; both runs' snap 1 sits at the same elapsed $t-t_0\approx3\times10^{-5}$ Myr. Fixed by plotting state at every snapshot and flagging only the PdV proxy.

10 · Reproduce

# failing repro + healthy control (ephemeral runs)
python run.py docs/dev/failed-large-clouds/harness/params/fail_repro.param
python run.py docs/dev/failed-large-clouds/harness/params/small_1e6.param

# figures + this report (from the committed CSVs / PNGs, no re-run needed)
python docs/dev/failed-large-clouds/figures/make_energy_budget_figs.py
python docs/dev/failed-large-clouds/figures/make_insights_html.py

# tests
pytest test/test_energy_collapse_guard.py

Chapter 2Heavy clouds, part 4 — can we keep them alive?

TRINITY feedback-bubble code · energy-driven phase · two diagnostic rescue experiments (H3, H4)

docs/dev/transition/pt4/ · point-in-time analysis (2026-06-22) · branch fix/transition-trigger-problem-pt4

⚠ These are point-in-time diagnostics, not a maintained spec. The source dev docs (H3_eb_floor_experiment.md, H4_pdvcap_experiment.md) carry the full three-paragraph staleness / living-plan / persist-diagnostics banners; treat every number and line reference here as unverified and re-check against current source before relying on it. Both experiments are monkeypatch-only — production code is untouched.

The previous chapter diagnosed why the heaviest clouds (mCloud=5e9) crash the energy phase: the bubble's thermal energy $E_b$ is drained by PdV expansion work faster than feedback supplies it, so it peaks, collapses through zero, and now terminates cleanly as ENERGY_COLLAPSED. This part 4 asks the natural follow-up: can we keep these clouds alive? Two maintainer hypotheses were tested as controlled, energy-injecting diagnostics — floor $E_b$ (H3), or cap the drain (H4). Neither can ship; both sharpen the diagnosis.

The control variable. Everything below tracks one ratio, $$\frac{\rm PdV}{L_{\rm mech}}=\frac{4\pi R_2^{2}\,P_b\,v_2}{L_{\rm mech}},$$ the expansion work the bubble does on its shell, relative to the mechanical (wind+SN) input. Above 1 the bubble loses energy net and $E_b$ falls; below 1 it grows. The whole heavy-cloud story is this ratio sitting above 1 for an extended early epoch.

1 · H3 — floor $E_b>0$ so the bubble keeps expanding?

The first idea is the most direct: if the run dies because $E_b$ collapses through zero, what if we simply floor $E_b$ to a small positive value so it never can? The EBFLOOR variant clamps $E_b=\max(E_b,\,10^{-3})$ at every consumer (so $P_b>0$, $R_1$ well-defined) and adds a reflecting floor on $dE_b/dt$ so the integrated state can only hold flat or grow at the floor. This injects energy and violates conservation — it is a diagnostic to isolate whether "$E_b\to0$" is the sole failure mode, not a fix candidate.

REFUTED $E_b$-collapse is NOT the sole failure mode. Forcing $E_b>0$ does not make either heavy cloud behave like a healthy one. The floor is a bit-identical no-op on 11 of 13 healthy/stall configs ($E_b$ never collapses there, so the branch is never taken); the other two differ only at the $\sim$10$^{-8}$ level. On the $5\times10^{9}\,M_\odot$ clouds it rescues neither — it exposes a second guard and a non-convergent grind instead.

What the floor exposes on the two real-collapse configs

Only the two $5\times10^{9}\,M_\odot$ runs actually collapse at these early times (mass_5e8/mass_1e9 stay healthy with $E_b>0$ in the window) — and they fail by two different shipped paths that the floor defeats neither:

configV0 (baseline)EBFLOORwhat the floor exposes
fail_repro
(mCloud=5e9, PISM=1e4, 1b)		 ENERGY_COLLAPSED (51): one implicit step drives $E_b:4.75\times10^{8}\to-9.14\times10^{8}$, clean stop at $t=0.00341$, $R_2=9.73$ pc. grind energy phase bit-identical to V0 (52/52 snapshots), but at the energy→implicit handoff the implicit solve_ivp hammers the RHS with $\sim$1.2M drive- + 0.6M state-clamps and never advances a single step; $R_2$ freezes at 8.61 pc, killed by the 300 s timeout. removing the clean $E_b\le0$ stop just exposes a non-convergent implicit grind — the energy-driven interior is genuinely degenerate as $E_b\to0$.
fail_helix
(real Helix: sfe=0.05, PISM=0, 1a)		 ENERGY_COLLAPSED (51) reached in phase 1a, $t=0.00255$, $R_2=7.03$ pc. identical stop byte-identical to V0 ($E_b=0.005085287954767937$ to the digit) — a second, independent guard fires. the floor clamps $E_b$ in bubble_E2P/solve_R1 but cannot stop the bubble-structure solve itself from degenerating as $E_b\to0$ (cooling table out of bounds, solve_R1 can't bracket) → "bubble solve degenerate as Eb -> 0".

A side-result worth keeping: the cooling-balance transition trigger never fires under EBFLOOR (the cooling-balance ratio rmin sits at 0.50–0.99 on every config, never within $100\times$ of the 0.05 threshold). Flooring $E_b$ adds drive, not cooling — it cannot manufacture a cooling-balance event. The collapse regime is also narrow and mass-gated: only $5\times10^{9}\,M_\odot$ collapses early.

2 · H4 — cap the PdV drain for an early window?

If the cause is PdV super-criticality, the next idea throttles the cause directly: during $t replace the drain with $\min({\rm PdV},\,\kappa\,L_{\rm mech})$ ($\kappa=0.9$), leaving the shell-acceleration drive untouched so the bubble still expands, then lift the cap. The question: does the bubble self-sustain after release, or re-collapse the moment PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ is unthrottled? Capping PdV under-drains the bubble / injects energy while active — again a diagnostic, not a fix.

SURVIVABLE TRANSIENT, NON-PHYSICAL RESCUE The heavy clouds are not instantaneously "stillborn" — there exists a window after which the bubble self-sustains — but the required window is longer than the natural PdV>1 epoch the real physics imposes, so the "rescue" is purchased entirely with injected, non-conserved energy.

The drain stays super-critical for an extended epoch

PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ stays above 1 for roughly $1.5$–$4.5\times10^{-3}$ Myr from the phase start, and the outcome of a transient cap depends entirely on whether the window outlasts that epoch. The maintainer's exact $\sim$10$^{-3}$ Myr is too short; the window splits the two clouds at $3\times10^{-3}$; and only a $10^{-2}$ Myr window keeps both alive:

$t_{\rm window}$ [Myr]fail_repro (1b, sfe=0.1)fail_helix (1a, sfe=0.05)
1e-3 collapses cap never even bites (PdV<$L_{\rm mech}$ that early); byte-identical to V0, collapses at $t=0.0034$. collapses cap lifts while PdV/$L\approx2.4$; same 1a stop ~$10^{-4}$ Myr later ($E_b\approx0.005$).
3e-3 re-collapses $E_b$ inflated to $7.9\times10^{9}$, dips hard at release, PdV/$L$ only crosses below 1 at $t\sim0.0046$ — too little, too late; ENERGY_COLLAPSED at $t=0.00515$. self-sustains $E_b$ grows under the cap; at release it dips but PdV/$L$ falls through 1 (1.09→1.00→0.94→0.88) and $E_b$ recovers — a genuine survivable transient.
1e-2 self-sustains but only after a full $10^{-2}$ Myr ($\sim$10$\times$ the proposed window) of injection has built $E_b\approx2.2\times10^{10}$ and dropped PdV/$L$ to 0.74 by release. self-sustains

The control parameter cleanly separates the regimes

The cap is a strict no-op on healthy/stall controls (simple_cluster, pl2_steep, small_1e6 all reach a clean STOPPING_TIME with max rel|$\Delta E_b$| $\le2.6\times10^{-5}$ vs V0) — a genuine no-op to $\ge4$ significant figures, the $\kappa=0.9$ graze being its only, negligible, honest cost. The reason is simple: on those configs PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ settles well below 1 once the self-similar phase is established (after a brief IC-handoff spike on the first step — a snapshot-proxy artifact that does not track the true $\dot E_b$, so the integrated cap stays a no-op), so the cap never meaningfully engages. The same single ratio that leaves the controls untouched is what crosses 1 and stays there to kill the $5\times10^{9}$ clouds.

3 · What it means — the heavy-cloud fix is momentum, not energy props

Both experiments converge on the same conclusion: propping up the energy phase does not revive these clouds. H3 shows that flooring $E_b$ only swaps a clean stop for a grind (fail_repro) or leaves a second degeneracy guard to fire (fail_helix) — the energy-driven interior is genuinely degenerate as $E_b\to0$ along more than one axis. H4 shows that the only way to keep the bubble alive is to manufacture an $E_b$ reservoir the real expansion work would have removed; the "rescue" is exactly the injected, non-conserved energy ${\rm PdV}-\kappa L_{\rm mech}$ integrated over the window.

The honest fate is a momentum-driven continuation (or added cooling/leakage physics), not an energy-phase prop. The shipped ENERGY_COLLAPSED termination is diagnosing a real physics breakdown, not hiding a tractable continuation. Whatever replaces it must hand off to a momentum phase when the energy-driven interior over-drains — the regime input H4 quantifies (extended PdV super-criticality) is precisely what such a fix needs.

The unifying picture: opposite extremes of one ratio

This closes the loop with the rest of the storyline. The normal-cloud stall (transition trigger never fires; the bubble stays energy-driven but never reaches the cooling-balance event) and the $5\times10^{9}$ crash (PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ crosses 1 and $E_b$ collapses) are opposite extremes of the same PdV/$L_{\rm mech}$ axis: stall clouds never over-drain (ratio stays comfortably below 1, the bubble lives but the trigger sleeps); heavy clouds over-drain hard (ratio rises through 1, the bubble dies). A single physical control parameter spans both pathologies — and points at the same handoff machinery as the cure.

The momentum handoff itself — the deferred family T, including the $E_b$-peak detection that would mark where the energy-driven phase should end and the momentum-driven continuation should begin — is the subject of the ongoing transition work (docs/dev/transition/). H3 and H4 are the diagnostics that justify pursuing it rather than patching the energy phase.

4 · Reproduce

# H3 Eb-floor matrix + analysis (monkeypatch-only; production untouched)
bash docs/dev/transition/pt4/h3_run_matrix.sh
python docs/dev/transition/pt4/h3_analyze.py

# H4 PdV-cap sweep + analysis
bash docs/dev/transition/pt4/h4_run_matrix.sh
python docs/dev/transition/pt4/h4_analyze.py

# figures + this report (from the committed CSVs / PNGs, no re-run needed)
python docs/dev/transition/pt4/make_pt4_figures.py   # h3_ebfloor_noop_and_grind, h4_control_vs_collapse
python docs/dev/transition/pt4/h4_figures.py         # h4_Eb_sweep_*, h4_pdvratio_sweep_*, h4_summary
python docs/dev/transition/pt4/make_pt4_heavy_report.py
